headline: Michigan's ground game overwhelms UCLA despite Iamaleava's passing brilliance
body: Nico Iamaleava put on a clinic through the air, completing 30 of 46 passes for 368 yards and four touchdowns, yet it wasn't enough to overcome Michigan's relentless commitment to running the football. The Bruins QB deserved better than a 23-point defeat, especially considering his efficiency and the fact that UCLA kept pace in total yardage. But Michigan's 183 rushing yards, powered by a more balanced attack, dictated tempo in ways that ultimately wore down the UCLA defense.

The turning point materialized in field position and execution rather than any single play. Michigan's running back Brock ground out 80 yards on just eight carries while adding two rushing touchdowns, a bruising complement to QB Browne's more modest 23-for-33 passing line. UCLA's two fumbles—both lost—provided Michigan with short fields at critical junctures, and the Wolverines converted those mistakes into touchdowns rather than field goals. Browne threw only two touchdown passes compared to Iamaleava's four, yet Michigan's rushing TDs made the difference in the run-heavy calculus of Week 2 football.

What made this loss particularly stinging for UCLA was that the passing game worked. Harris caught five passes for 86 yards and a score, and the Bruins moved the ball enough to suggest they belonged in this game. Instead, they finished a touchdown on each turnover short of what they needed. Michigan's defense, anchored by Blackburn's seven tackles, bent without breaking and forced the issue when it mattered most.
turningPoint: UCLA's two fumbles, both recovered by Michigan, shifted momentum decisively as the Wolverines converted short fields into rushing touchdowns that UCLA could not match.

unsungHero.name: Brock
unsungHero.why: His two rushing touchdowns and efficient ground game established Michigan's physical dominance, yet Browne's modest passing stats will overshadow the running back's direct impact on the scoreboard.
